That's not accurate. `include_dirs` takes a list of paths. Setuptools itself produces the `-I` directives to the compiler (and the directive depends on the compiler). You can see the build output by running `python setup.py build`.

Yes, with this `/usr/local` will come first. It can always be overriden with the `--include-dirs (-I)` built in setuptools option. See `python setup.py build_ext -h`.

`zope.testbrowser` 5 does neither have `PublisherConnection` nor `PublisherResponse` in [testing.py](https://github.com/zopefoundation/zope.testbrowser/blob/master/src/zope/testbrowser/testing.py). This means that the whole `_z2_testbrowser.py` module will not work with `zope.testbrowser` >= 5.0
The needed imports in `zope.testbrowser` were removed in [f01ac733b](https://github.com/zopefoundation/zope.testbrowser/commit/f01ac733b04036ff6bdbec944ca592a47a5e45c3#diff-69501ce4c6ec40f61f5fa8a2277baf4d) but the imported code still exists in [zope.app.testing](https://github.com/zopefoundation/zope.app.testing/blob/master/src/zope/app/testing/testbrowser.py) but it will not run with `zope.testbrowser` >= 5 as it still depends on `mechanize` which was removed in `zope.testbrowser` 5.0.

I am not sure who uses (aka can use) this module at all. (It requires to use `zope.app.testing` which IMHO is not that common in Zope 2 land and it can only work with an older `zope.testbrowser` version. But I do not know what plone does. I think the needed bits to run `zope.testbrowser` on Zope 2 or 4 have been moved to [Zope master](https://github.com/zopefoundation/Zope/blob/master/src/Testing/testbrowser.py) resp. [2.13 branch](https://github.com/zopefoundation/Zope/blob/2.13/src/Testing/testbrowser.py))

I'd suggest to remove this `_z2_testbrowser.py` module in another PR and release this as `plone.testing` 6.0.

Yeah, the cause here is that the DOS CLI does not really have a notion of executable files. As far as it concerns, everything can be launched, and it’s up to the operating system to decide what to do. As a consequence, `where.exe` find all sorts of things, not just applications. It would happily return `django-admin.py` if it’s in `PATH`, and `subprocess.Popen` (actually the `CreateProcess` API it uses under the hood) would be hung to dry.

So the root issue here is that we are mixing two paradigms when implementing Pipenv for Windows because the deep roots of Windows lacking a real user-facing “executable” flag. I’d doubt even a re-implemented `which` function would help, unless you dig deep into Windows API to actually decide if a file is a real application.

> If operators with different priorities are used, consider adding whitespace around the operators with the lowest priority(ies). Use your own judgment; however, never use more than one space, and always have the same amount of whitespace on both sides of a binary operator.

> ```
> Yes:
> 
> i = i + 1
> submitted += 1
> x = x*2 - 1
> hypot2 = x*x + y*y
> c = (a+b) * (a-b)
> No:
> 
> i=i+1
> submitted +=1
> x = x * 2 - 1
> hypot2 = x * x + y * y
> c = (a + b) * (a - b)
> ```

yes, the problem is that there are various meanings of "error"... IMO they don't mean to remove the whole result row / cancel the whole query, but just the one binding within the row, as shown here:
http://www.w3.org/TR/sparql11-query/#aggregateExample2

So if we encounter such an error, i think it's correct to somehow return the row with a placeholder indicating an unbound variable (which i thought `None` was), rather than removing the result row or simply cancelling the whole query.

At least that's what Virtuoso does in the sample query above, so it must be true ;)

Again, I think you can simplify this code considerably by making use of the `CelestialCoord.angleBetween` function.  If I followed what you are doing here, you want the spherical angle between `pos`, `crval` and `north = galsim.CelestialCoord(0*galsim.degrees, 90*galsim.degrees)`.  Then 
```
pa_sa = pos.angleBetween(crval, north)
```
Or maybe the reverse.  Not sure about the sign convention here.  But that code does this in a more robust way that avoids all these checks about sin > 1 or straddling 90 degrees, etc.

I think this is another thing to come back to if there's a demonstrated need for it.

Currently, there are two things that might fail and thus be retried.  A failed message write is probably on account of a broken pipe.  It's retried with a new connection.  Assuming the new connection was successfully obtained, there's no reason the second attempt shouldn't succeed.  So on that front, I'm not sure if we need to get any fancier... at least for now.

The other thing that gets retried are failed dialing attempts.  That's currently hardcoded through a constant to be retried five times before giving up, at which point there isn't a prayer of firing off a log message to the external service, so the drain is temporarily muted.

Muting a log drain has the benefit of (forgive the overloaded term here) _draining_ the internal message queue very quickly so that when things recover, we're draining recent log messages out to the external log archive instead of working through a backlog of older log messages while newer log messages get dropped because of the backed up queue.

I _think_ this all sounds pretty reasonable, but I am more than willing to hear further challenges to that.

this isnt related to channels and is a completely new feature. Let me give an example to make this scenario clear - 
assume that a bot developer x has built a bot that lets you see and manage your `github` repositories. at the start of a conversation, the bot will require you to sign in into your `github`. however you (as a malignant user) can trick someone else (like me) by sending me this sign in link. if i signin, the bot will think it's your repos and now you can do anything you want with my github account.
this new feature helps protect against this scenario. the cli portion of it lets the bot developer register his github (or any other service provider) app credentials with botservice, so that we can secure the above transaction . previously this security would be on them to implement.
there are around 53 service providers that we support and each has its own fields and the way they implement scopes is different. This is why this information has not been captured in the swagger (also the bot developer knows what the separator would be ).

In Python, the default value will be instantiated once and reused. And append/extend on a list is a destructive operations, so the default value itself (for further invocations) will be updated. For example:
```python
def foo(x, y=[]):
    y.append(x)
    return y

foo(1)  # => [1]
foo(2)  # => [1, 2]
```
